prompt: Use the PAS (Problem-Agitate-Solve) copywriting formula to write a 250-300 word opening for an opinion article arguing 'Most engineering teams under 50 people don't need a dedicated observability stack.' Audience: engineering managers at small Series A-B companies. Save the opening to opening.md in the current working directory. The opening should clearly demonstrate the three PAS phases. Don't ask follow-up questions.
assertions: opening.md contains an Agitate phase with at least ONE specific quantified cost — a number paired with a unit and an implication. Examples that count: 'engineers spending 6 hours per week on dashboards', '$X per month for tools nobody opens', 'a 3-month onboarding tax'. Pure adjectives ('expensive', 'time-consuming', 'overwhelming') without numbers do NOT count, AND the Agitate phase must mention at least one mechanism for the cost (e.g. 'compounds', 'because', 'leads to', 'on top of', 'pulls engineers from') — not just a one-line cost claim.

opening.md uses first-person stake or named-team specificity at least once — the literal words 'I', 'we', 'our', 'I\'ve seen', 'in my experience', or a named company/team — to signal lived authority. A purely third-person detached opening ('many teams find that...', 'the industry has long believed') does NOT satisfy this.

opening.md AVOIDS dishonest agitation: it must NOT contain ALL CAPS WORDS for emphasis (any word ≥4 characters in all uppercase, excluding obvious acronyms like 'PAS', 'CI', 'CEO', 'OKR', 'SaaS', 'AWS', 'GCP', 'OSS', 'FAANG'), AND it must NOT contain any of these manipulation phrases (case-insensitive): 'shocking', 'you won\'t believe', 'the truth that', 'experts hate', 'industry secret', 'never recover', 'one weird trick', 'this changes everything'.
